summary.name: Vasaka (Malabar Nut)
summary.scientific_name: Adhatoda vasica Nees (syn. Justicia adhatoda L.)

plant_profile.history: Unique History: Vasaka (meaning 'the one that smells' or 'the one that grows abundantly') has been central to Ayurvedic respiratory medicine since its earliest documentation in the Charaka Samhita and Sushruta Samhita. Its significance is underscored by the fact that its principal alkaloid, Vasicine, served as the direct chemical inspiration for the semi-synthetic drug Bromhexine (marketed as Bisolvon), one of the world's most prescribed mucolytics. This represents one of the most direct and successful reverse-pharmacology validations of an Ayurvedic herb. Physical Characteristics: An evergreen, densely branched, large perennial shrub, growing up to 1-2.5 meters tall. The leaves are large, simple, opposite, lanceolate to ovate-lanceolate, 10-20 cm long, with a smooth upper surface and slightly downy undersurface, possessing a characteristic unpleasant taste when chewed. Flowers are white to pale lilac/pink, with purple/violet stripes on the lower lip, arranged in dense terminal or axillary spikes. The fruit is a clavate capsule containing 4 seeds. It is widely distributed throughout the plains and lower Himalayan regions of India, Sri Lanka, Myanmar, and Malaysia, often growing in wastelands and as hedges. Specific Chemical Compounds: The alkaloid profile is dominated by quinazoline alkaloids: Vasicine (Peganine) is the primary alkaloid (constituting 1-2% of dried leaf weight), responsible for bronchodilatory and mucolytic effects. Vasicinone is the auto-oxidation product of Vasicine and exhibits additional bronchodilatory and respiratory stimulant properties. Deoxyvasicine has uterotonic and abortifacient properties. Other constituents include adhatodic acid, vasicol, vasicinol, beta-sitosterol, and essential oil. The ratio of Vasicine to Vasicinone is critical for the overall pharmacological activity and is a key quality parameter. Strict Standardizations: As per the Ayurvedic Pharmacopoeia of India (API), the leaves (fresh and dried) and root of Adhatoda vasica are the standardized parts. Quality parameters include total ash, acid-insoluble ash, alcohol-soluble extractive, water-soluble extractive, and foreign matter limits. Quantitative estimation of total alkaloid content (calculated as Vasicine) is mandatory, typically requiring not less than 0.5% total alkaloids in the dried leaf. HPTLC/HPLC methods using Vasicine as the primary marker compound are employed for quality assurance and authentication, aligning with WHO and Indian Pharmacopoeia guidelines.

ayurvedic_properties.dosha_modulation: Primarily pacifies Kapha and Pitta doshas. Its Tikta (bitter) Rasa and Sheeta (cooling) Virya make it the supreme Pitta-Kapha pacifier, particularly for conditions involving heat and mucus in the respiratory system. May slightly aggravate Vata due to its Ruksha (dry) and Laghu (light) Gunas.

medicinal_uses.bronchodilatory_and_anti_asthmatic: Vasaka is the single most important Ayurvedic herb for respiratory diseases. Vasicine (a quinazoline alkaloid) is a potent bronchodilator that relaxes the smooth muscles of the bronchi, providing relief in asthma and bronchospasm. This pharmacological activity is so well-validated that Vasicine served as the chemical template for the development of the modern synthetic drug Bromhexine (the world's most widely used mucolytic).
medicinal_uses.expectorant_and_mucolytic__kaphaghna_: Vasicine and Vasicinone powerfully liquefy thick, tenacious bronchial mucus and stimulate the mucociliary escalator, facilitating the expulsion of phlegm. This makes Vasaka the herb of choice for productive cough with thick, difficult-to-expectorate sputum.
medicinal_uses.hemostatic___anti_hemorrhagic__raktapitt: One of Vasaka's most unique and invaluable actions. It is the premier Ayurvedic remedy for Raktapitta—bleeding from any orifice of the body, especially hemoptysis (coughing up blood from the lungs). Its cooling Virya constricts blood vessels and its astringent Rasa helps coagulate blood.
medicinal_uses.anti_tubercular: Traditional Ayurveda has used Vasaka for Rajayakshma (tuberculosis) for centuries. Modern research has confirmed that Vasicine and its derivatives possess anti-mycobacterial activity against Mycobacterium tuberculosis.
medicinal_uses.uterotonic_and_anti_hemorrhagic_in_gynec: Used to manage menorrhagia (excessive uterine bleeding) and post-partum hemorrhage. Its hemostatic (Raktasthambhana) properties combined with its mild uterotonic action make it clinically valuable in Ayurvedic gynecology.
medicinal_uses.chemical_constituents: Contains quinazoline alkaloids (Vasicine/Peganine at 1-2% of dried leaf weight being the principal alkaloid, Vasicinone—the auto-oxidation product of Vasicine with its own distinct pharmacological activity, Deoxyvasicine, Maiontone, Vasicinol), essential oil, betaine, steroids (daucosterol), alkanes, fatty acids, and vitamin C. Vasicine is the primary marker compound and the bioactive entity responsible for the majority of its respiratory pharmacological actions.

preparation_methods.fresh_leaf_juice__svarasa_: Crush fresh mature Vasaka leaves and extract the juice. This is the most potent and traditionally preferred form. Dose: 10-20 ml, typically mixed with an equal quantity of raw honey (Madhu) for cough and bronchitis. Honey acts as both a Yogavahi (bioavailability enhancer) and a synergistic anti-tussive agent.
preparation_methods.vasavaleha__medicated_confection_avaleha: A classical Ayurvedic preparation made from Vasaka leaf juice cooked with sugar, ghee, honey, Pippali (long pepper), and other herbs. This is a palatable, shelf-stable form widely prescribed for chronic cough, asthma, and tuberculosis. Prepared as per AFI guidelines.
preparation_methods.vasarishta___vasakasava__fermented_prepa: Classical fermented liquid preparations using Vasaka as the primary herb, combined with other respiratory herbs, jaggery, and fermentation agents. These are long-acting formulations for chronic respiratory conditions.
preparation_methods.powder__churna_: Shade-dried leaves are finely powdered and sieved. Less potent than the fresh juice but convenient. Dose: 1-3g with honey or warm water.
preparation_methods.decoction__kwatha_: 10-15g of dried Vasaka leaves are boiled in 200ml of water until reduced to 50ml. Used for acute cough and fever.

dosage.adults: Fresh Leaf Juice (Svarasa): 10-20 ml, 2-3 times daily with honey. Dried Leaf Powder (Churna): 1-3 grams per day. Vasavaleha: 5-10 grams, 1-2 times daily with warm milk or water. Vasarishta/Vasakasava: 15-30 ml with equal water, twice daily after meals.
dosage.children: Fresh Juice (Svarasa): 5-10 ml with honey, 1-2 times daily (for children above 5 years). Vasavaleha: 2-5g with warm milk, 1-2 times daily (as per label for pediatric preparations). Use under strict medical supervision due to the presence of Deoxyvasicine.
dosage.elderly: Standard adult dosages, but start at the lower end. Monitor for any excessive drying effects (Vata aggravation). It is highly beneficial for age-related chronic bronchitis and COPD. Combine with a Vata-pacifying herb if needed.

contraindications: Vasaka is strictly contraindicated in pregnancy due to the presence of Deoxyvasicine, which has documented abortifacient and uterotonic activity. It should be avoided in individuals with active peptic ulcers or severe gastritis, as its bitterness can increase gastric motility. Caution in individuals with severe Vata aggravation (extreme dryness) as its Ruksha and Laghu properties can further deplete body fluids.
side_effects: Generally safe at therapeutic doses. High doses may cause nausea, vomiting (it is traditionally classified as a Vamana Dravya/emetic at higher doses), or diarrhea. Prolonged high-dose use may lead to excessive respiratory tract dryness in some individuals. The bitter taste can cause mild gastric irritation.
drug_interactions: May potentiate the effects of other mucolytic/expectorant drugs (e.g., Bromhexine, Ambroxol, Guaifenesin) due to its own potent mucolytic action. Potential interaction with antitubercular drugs (additive anti-mycobacterial effects). Caution with anticoagulants, as some studies suggest mild anti-platelet activity. Monitor if co-administered with drugs metabolized by CYP450 enzymes.
safety.toxicity_notes: GRAS Status: Vasaka has a very long and well-documented history of safe traditional use in Ayurvedic medicine for respiratory conditions. It is listed in the API, Indian Pharmacopoeia, and British Pharmacopoeia. WHO acknowledges its traditional applications. It is safe at recommended therapeutic doses in non-pregnant adults.
